This avoids double-counting the same measure in multiple districts. - **Totals:** About **$1.55 billion** in voter-approved local conservation funds were approved in California since 2010; roughly **$1.48 billion** could be geographically allocated to CDs. The remaining ~$97 million comes from a couple of very small jurisdiction geometries that do not intersect any 2024 CD polygon. - **Boundary note:** Ballot measures occurred under earlier district maps; I used current 2024 boundaries for consistency. ## Answer: **141 seamounts**  The Sargasso Sea EBSA (`GLOBAL_ID = 'WC_13'`, described at the CBD Wider Caribbean and Western Mid-Atlantic workshop) contains **141 seamount polygons** (distinct `_cng_fid` values with `feature_type = 'Seamounts'` in the Harris et al. 2014 seafloor geomorphology dataset).  ### Context  | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Sargasso Sea EBSA total area | ~4,168,000 km² | | EBSA portion on the high seas | ~3,699,000 km² (88.8%) | | EBSA portion within EEZs | ~468,000 km² (11.2%) | | Seamounts within the EBSA | **141** |  The EBSA is predominantly on the high seas (~89%), with the remaining ~11% overlapping EEZs (primarily Bermuda, and to a lesser extent the US, Bahamas, and other nearby jurisdictions). The seamount count above is for the full EBSA extent as designated.  ### Important caveats  1. **EBSAs are not protected areas.** The Sargasso Sea EBSA is a *scientific description* under the Convention on Biological Diversity — it carries no legal protection or management measures. (The nearby Sargasso Sea Commission is a separate, voluntary arrangement by the surrounding governments.) 2. **Seamount count is polygon-based.** The 141 figure counts distinct seamount *polygons* in the Harris et al. (2014) geomorphology map. Individual seamounts can be represented by multiple polygons (e.g., a summit polygon and a base polygon), so this may differ from a count of "individual seamounts." The dataset also doesn't include guyots separately — those are tagged `feature_type = 'Guyots'` and would add more features if included. 3. **Hex-based join at h6.** The join used the EBSA's pre-computed `h6` parent column against the seafloor-geomorphology hex (native resolution 6). This is a "found within" spatial match — seamounts whose h6 cells overlap the EBSA's h6 cells are counted. A polygon-level `ST_Intersects` check would give a more precise count if needed.

## Short answer  Designating all currently mapped EBSAs as no‑take MPAs on the **high‑seas** portion would close **~42.7 million km²** of ocean and displace roughly **1.9 million hours of apparent fishing effort per year** in the most recent data (2024). That is about **20% of all high‑seas fishing hours** and **~1.7% of global fishing hours**.  The displaced effort is dominated by **drifting longlines**, followed by **squid jiggers** and unclassified/mixed fishing.  ---  ## 1. Spatial footprint  | Metric | Value | |---|---| | High‑seas EBSA area (exact H3 cell area) | **42.7 M km²** | | Share of all high‑seas area (IHO/Marine Regions v2) | **~19%** | | Share of total EBSA area in this layer | **~62%** | | Number of EBSAs in this layer | 203 |  The rest of the mapped EBSAs lie inside EEZs and are therefore excluded when you ask specifically about the high seas.  ---  ## 2. Fishing effort displaced (2024)  Using Global Fishing Watch v3 (AIS‑derived) 2024 effort:  | Gear type | Displaced fishing hours | Share | |---|---:|---:| | Drifting longlines | **1,444,760** | **75.0%** | | Squid jigger | **275,679** | **14.3%** | | Fishing (unclassified/mixed) | **131,475** | **6.8%** | | Trawlers | **30,554** | **1.6%** | | Pole and line | **27,390** | **1.4%** | | Tuna purse seines | **10,746** | **0.6%** | | Fixed gear | **3,131** | **0.2%** | | Set longlines | **2,924** | **0.2%** | | Purse seines, trollers, pots/traps | **~490** | **<0.1%** | | **Total** | **~1,927,000** | **100%** |  So the main displaced **industrial** fisheries would be:  - **Drifting longlines** — high‑seas tuna, billfish and swordfish longline fleets. - **Squid jiggers** — industrial jigging vessels targeting pelagic squid. - **Trawlers / purse‑seine / pole‑and‑line** — smaller but non‑negligible shares of bottom/midwater trawling and tuna purse‑seine/pole‑and‑line operations.  ---  ## 3. Context relative to all high‑seas fishing  | Year | Total high‑seas fishing hours | EBSA high‑seas displaced hours | EBSA share of high seas | |------|---:|---:|---:| | 2012 | 1.45 M | 0.35 M | ~24% | | 2016 | 5.68 M | 1.42 M | ~25% | | 2018 | 8.19 M | 2.40 M | ~29% | | 2020 | 8.93 M | 2.08 M | ~23% | | 2022 | 10.12 M | 2.03 M | ~20% | | **2024** | **9.75 M** | **1.93 M** | **~19.8%** |  - **2024 displaced effort** ≈ **1.9 M hours** - **Average 2018‑2024** ≈ **2.06 M hours/year** - **Share of 2024 global effort** ≈ **1.7%**  Early years (2012‑2014) look artificially low because AIS coverage and GFW detection were still ramping up, so the 2018‑2024 average is a better “current‑effort” benchmark.  ---  ## 4. How the numbers were derived  1. **High‑seas EBSAs** = every EBSA hex cell (`h8`) whose parent `h4` cell falls in the IHO/Marine Regions high‑seas mask. 2. **Area** = exact `h3_cell_area(h8, 'km²')` summed over the distinct high‑seas EBSA cells. 3. **Fishing effort** = GFW is at `h6` (~36 km²). For each `h6` cell overlapping the high‑seas EBSAs, effort was apportioned by the **fraction of its `h8` sub‑cells that are inside those EBSAs** (area‑proportional; treating effort as uniformly distributed within each `h6` cell). 4. The whole‑cell alternative (assigning the entire `h6` effort whenever any part of the cell is inside an EBSA) gives ~1.96 M hours, so the area‑proportional estimate is robust.  ---  ## 5. Important caveats  - **EBSA coverage is incomplete**: the layer contains only 203 EBSAs from 9 of ~15 CBD regional workshops. Regions such as the North‑East Atlantic, Baltic, Black Sea/Caspian, East Asian seas and North Indian Ocean are missing. Summed `area_ha_x10/10` per basin → **hectares of wetland**.  3. **Vulnerable Carbon**: From `vulnerable-carbon-2024` (H3 res-9, Mg C per cell). Rolled up to h8 via `h3_cell_to_parent(h9,8)`, summed per basin → **Mg C (tonnes)**, converted to **Mt C**.  4. **NCP (Nature's Contributions to People)**: From `ncp_biod_nathab` (H3 res-7, 0–20 intensity). California has **26.47 million acres** of land conserved at GAP status 1 or 2, according to the 2025 CA Nature Terrestrial Conserved Areas layer.  This figure was computed by de-duplicating the conserved-area features (one feature may be tiled across many hex cells) and summing the `Acres` column, which already represents the GAP 1+2 portion of each unit.

**Mojave Desert** has the most conserved acreage.  Using the 2025 California Terrestrial 30x30 Conserved Areas layer and grouping by each unit’s assigned ecoregion (`CA_Ecoregi`):  | Rank | Ecoregion | GAP 1+2 acres (30x30-counted) | Total protected acres (GAP 1–4) | |---:|:---|---:|---:| | 1 | **Mojave Desert** | **7,373,398** | **10,937,602** | | 2 | Sierra Nevada | 4,638,088 | 9,990,180 | | 3 | Southern California Mountains and Valleys | 2,479,494 | 4,323,016 | | 4 | Southeastern Great Basin | 1,988,871 | 2,378,667 | | 5 | Sonoran Desert | 1,626,090 | 2,504,237 |  So the Mojave Desert leads both in acreage counted toward the 30x30 goal (GAP 1 + GAP 2) and in total protected acreage (all GAP statuses combined).

About **16.5% of global land** is currently inside a designated protected area (WDPA).  | Metric | Land cells (h8) | % of global land | |---|---:|---:| | WDPA protected areas only | 29.3 million | **16.5%** | | WDPA + OECMs (Target 3 “conserved” definition) | 31.1 million | **17.5%** |  ### How this was calculated - **Protected area source:** WDPA (June 2026 edition). - **Land mask:** Copernicus Global Land Cover 2019, treating a cell as land when `lc_class NOT IN (0, 80, 200)` — i.e. excluding no-data, permanent water, and open sea. - **Resolution:** H3 resolution 8 (the coarsest resolution at which WDPA guarantees complete feature coverage). - **Method:** Count distinct land h8 cells that overlap any WDPA h8 cell; same for WDPA ∪ WD-OECM.  So the gap to the 30×30 target is roughly **13.5 percentage points** on land if counting protected areas alone, or **12.5 percentage points** if OECMs are included.

Accordingly, the vast majority of its seamounts (129 of 141) are in areas beyond national jurisdiction, where they fall under the BBNJ (High Seas Treaty) framework rather than national protection.  **Data sources:** Seamounts from the Harris et al. ## Summary  **Ramsar Criterion 9** — *"A wetland should be considered internationally important if it regularly supports 1% or more of the population of a species or subspecies of waterbird that is considered to be at risk of extinction, or 1% or more of the population of a species or subspecies of waterbird that is considered to be declining."*  In plain terms: it's the **1% population threshold, but restricted to waterbird species of conservation concern** — IUCN Vulnerable/Endangered/Critically Endangered, or those in documented decline. About **220 million people** live inside the mapped boundaries of the world’s protected areas, according to the latest data.  | Dataset / definition | People inside (2020) | Share of world population | |---|---:|---:| | **WDPA protected areas (all statuses)** | **~220 million** | **~2.8%** | | WDPA — excluding “Proposed” sites | ~213 million | ~2.7% | | **WDPA + OECMs** (CBD Target 3 “conserved” areas) | ~249 million | ~3.2% |  ### How this was estimated - **Protected-area source:** WDPA June 2026 (`s3://public-wdpa/wdpa/hex/…`), with ~306,000 features. - **Population source:** GHS-POP 2020 (`s3://public-population/ghs-pop-2020/hex/…`), totaling ~7.84 billion people globally. - **Method:** H3 hex join. WDPA uses adaptive resolution:   - Smaller protected areas are tiled at **H3 resolution 9** (~0.10 km²), so population is summed at that fine scale.   - Very large protected areas (≥5,000 km²) are tiled at **H3 resolution 8** (~0.74 km²), so population is summed at that coarser scale.   - Overlapping protected areas are de-duplicated by counting each H3 cell only once.  ### Caveats - Boundary cells are assigned entirely to a protected area if the H3 cell is classified as inside it, so the true figure could be slightly higher or lower. - Marine protected areas are included in WDPA but contribute essentially zero people, so the result reflects terrestrial/coastal population. - OECMs are counted separately under the CBD Kunming-Montreal Target 3 definition; protected areas alone (WDPA) are what most people mean by “existing protected areas.”  Would you like this broken down by country, IUCN category, or visualized on the map?

You can click on any site to see its details.  ---  ## What is Ramsar Criterion 9?  **Criterion 9** (added in 2005 at COP9) states:  > *"A wetland should be considered internationally important if it regularly supports 1% or more of a biogeographic population of a non-avian animal species."*  ### Key points: - **Focus on non-avian species** — The original 8 criteria (1971) were heavily oriented toward waterbirds. Criterion 9 was created to recognize wetlands critical for **fish, mammals, reptiles, amphibians, invertebrates, and other non-bird taxa**. - **1% threshold** — The site must regularly support at least 1% of a *biogeographic population* (not global population) of a species. - **Regular support** — The species must use the wetland consistently, not just occasionally. - **Examples of species triggering Criterion 9**:    - Fish migrations (e.g., sturgeon, salmon)   - Marine mammals (manatees, dolphins in estuaries)   - Freshwater turtles, crocodilians   - Amphibian breeding aggregations   - Invertebrate populations (e.g., freshwater mussels, crustaceans)  ### Why it matters: Many of the largest sites in the list above (Río Yata, Río Blanco, Kakadu, Bas Ogooué, Banc d'Arguin) are vast floodplains, deltas, or coastal systems that serve as critical nursery grounds, migration corridors, or refuge habitats for non-avian species at population-significant scales. I'll calculate the vulnerable carbon (2024) stored in India's wetlands by joining the GLWD v2 wetland classes with the vulnerable carbon hex data, filtered to India's boundary. The carbon is reported in million tonnes of carbon (Mt C).  ```sql SET THREADS=100; SET preserve_insertion_order=false; SET enable_object_cache=true; INSTALL httpfs; LOAD httpfs; INSTALL h3 FROM community; LOAD h3; CREATE OR REPLACE SECRET s3 (TYPE S3, ENDPOINT 'rook-ceph-rgw-nautiluss3.rook',     URL_STYLE 'path', USE_SSL 'false', KEY_ID '', SECRET '');  WITH india AS (   SELECT DISTINCT h8, h0   FROM read_parquet('s3://public-overturemaps/2026-02-18.0/countries/hex/h0=*/data_0.parquet')   WHERE country = 'IN' ), wetlands AS (   SELECT DISTINCT g.h8, g.h0, g.Z   FROM read_parquet('s3://public-wetlands/glwd/hex/h0=*/data_0.parquet') g   JOIN india i ON g.h8 = i.h8 AND g.h0 = i.h0 ), carbon_by_h8 AS (   SELECT c.h8, c.h0, SUM(c.carbon) AS carbon_mg   FROM read_parquet('s3://public-carbon/vulnerable-carbon-2024/hex/h0=*/data_0.parquet') c   JOIN wetlands w ON c.h8 = w.h8 AND c.h0 = w.h0   GROUP BY c.h8, c.h0 ) SELECT    w.Z,   cat.name AS wetland_type,   cat.category AS wetland_category,   SUM(c.carbon_mg) / 1e6 AS vulnerable_carbon_million_tonnes,   ROUND(100.0 * SUM(c.carbon_mg) / SUM(SUM(c.carbon_mg)) OVER (), 2) AS pct_of_total FROM wetlands w JOIN carbon_by_h8 c ON w.h8 = c.h8 AND w.h0 = c.h0 JOIN read_csv_auto('s3://public-wetlands/glwd/category_codes.csv') cat ON w.Z = cat.Z GROUP BY w.Z, cat.name, cat.category ORDER BY vulnerable_carbon_million_tonnes DESC; ```

Tropical Pacific) | 160,150 | 10% | | Agulhas Front | 120,617 | 8% | | Central Indian Basin | 90,957 | 6% |  ## Important caveats  1. **EBSAs are not protected areas.** They are CBD scientific descriptions and carry no legal restrictions. This analysis assumes a hypothetical closure. 2. **Coverage is incomplete.** The EBSA layer contains only 203 areas from 9 of ~15 CBD regional workshops. Regions such as the North-East Atlantic, Baltic, Black Sea & Caspian, Seas of East Asia, and North Indian Ocean are missing. The estimate is therefore a lower bound. 3. **AIS-only, industrial-scale fishing.** Global Fishing Watch tracks vessels broadcasting AIS. Small-scale, non-AIS, and illegal/unreported fishing are not included. 4. **High-seas boundary sensitivity.** I used the IHO/Marine Regions high-seas mask. If instead you define “high seas” by subtracting EEZ cells at H3 resolution-8, the displaced effort rises to **~24.2 million hours (2012–2024)** and the area to **~43.5 million km²** — about **18% higher** fishing effort, mainly because the coarse h4 high-seas mask misses some high-seas cells near EEZ boundaries. 5. **Effort ≠ catch or value.** “Fishing hours” is a measure of vessel activity, not landed tonnage or economic value.  In short: closing all high-seas portions of currently described EBSAs would displace **roughly 1.6 million hours of industrial fishing per year**, concentrated in **longline and squid-jigging fleets** in the central Pacific, Indian Ocean, and equatorial productivity zones.

## Summary  ### 🌍 Ramsar Criterion 9 — Explained  **Criterion 9** is one of nine criteria used to identify **Wetlands of International Importance** under the Ramsar Convention. It reads:  > *"A wetland should be considered internationally important if it regularly supports **1% of the individuals in a population of one species or subspecies of waterbird**, or 1% of the individuals in a population of one species or subspecies of animal dependent upon wetlands."*  This is the **"1% population threshold"** rule — a quantitative benchmark. If a wetland regularly hosts at least 1% of a global or biogeographic population of a single waterbird (or wetland-dependent animal) species, it qualifies. The threshold is set deliberately low because it identifies sites that are **critical to species survival** — losing them would push populations toward extinction risk.  ### 📊 Global Statistics for Criterion 9 Sites  | Metric | Value | |---|---| | **Distinct Ramsar sites meeting Criterion 9** | **52** | | **Total H3 hex cells covered** | 163,039 | | **Total area** | ~799,900 km² |  ### 🏆 Top Countries by Criterion 9 Sites  | Country | Sites | |---|---| | 🇦🇺 Australia | 6 | | 🇺🇸 United States | 5 | | 🇬🇦 Gabon | 4 | | 🇫🇷 France | 4 | | 🇲🇽 Mexico | 3 | | 🇲🇬 Madagascar | 3 | | 🇳🇱 Netherlands | 2 | | 🇲🇦 Morocco | 2 | | 🇸🇪 Sweden | 2 | | 🇿🇲 Zambia | 2 |  ### 🗺️ Largest Criterion 9 Sites  | Site | Country | Area (ha) | |---|---|---| | Réserve naturelle nationale des Terres australes françaises | France | 2,337,100 | | Kakadu National Park | Australia | 1,979,770 | | Bas Ogooué | Gabon | 1,370,000 | | Parc National du Banc d'Arguin | Mauritania | 1,200,000 | | Zambezi Floodplains | Zambia | 900,000 | | Kafue Flats | Zambia | 600,500 | | Guaporé Biological Reserve | Brazil | 600,000 | | Îles Barren | Madagascar | 463,200 | | Wadden Sea | Netherlands | 271,023 | | Gough Island | UK | 243,561 |  ### 🗺️ Map View  I've displayed the Ramsar layer on the map, **filtered to only sites meeting Criterion 9**, styled in red, with a tooltip showing site name, country, area, and the Criterion 9 flag. ## Bottom line  If **all high-seas portions of the EBSAs in this layer** were closed to fishing, about **1.93 million hours of industrial fishing** would be displaced, based on Global Fishing Watch’s 2024 AIS-observed effort.  That is roughly:  - **1.7%** of all AIS-observed global fishing hours in 2024   - **~20%** of AIS-observed fishing hours on the high seas in 2024   - Concentrated in **just two gear types**: **drifting longlines** and **squid jiggers**, which together account for **~89%** of the displaced effort.  The high-seas EBSA footprint covers **~46.1 million km²** across **59 EBSAs** (the layer contains 203 EBSAs, but only 59 extend into ABNJ).  ---  ## Displaced fishing effort by gear type (2024)  | Gear type | Displaced fishing hours | Share of displaced total | |-----------|------------------------:|-------------------------:| | Drifting longlines | 1,444,800 | 75.0% | | Squid jigger | 275,700 | 14.3% | | Unclassified fishing (“fishing”) | 131,500 | 6.8% | | Trawlers | 30,600 | 1.6% | | Pole and line | 27,400 | 1.4% | | Tuna purse seines | 10,700 | 0.6% | | Fixed gear | 3,100 | 0.2% | | Set longlines | 2,900 | 0.2% | | Purse seines | 350 | <0.1% | | Trollers | 135 | <0.1% | | Pots and traps | 5 | <0.1% | | **Total** | **1,927,200** | **100%** |  So the displacement is dominated by **longline fisheries** (likely tunas, billfish and swordfish on the high seas) and **squid jigging**.  ---  ## How this was calculated  - **EBSA boundary**: `ebsa/hex/h0=*` at H3 resolution 8.   - **High-seas mask**: `iho/high-seas/hex/h0=*` at H3 resolution 4 (ABNJ = beyond EEZ).   - **Fishing effort**: Global Fishing Watch annual effort v3 at H3 resolution 6, for **2024**.  I selected every H3 res-8 EBSA cell whose res-4 parent lies in the high seas, then built a res-6 area mask. For each GFW res-6 cell that overlaps those high-seas EBSA cells, I area-weighted the fishing hours:  ``` displaced_hours = fishing_hours × (high-seas EBSA area inside this h6 cell / total h6 cell area) ```  This avoids double-counting where EBSAs overlap and gives an exact area-weighted estimate, assuming fishing effort is evenly distributed within each ~36 km² GFW cell.  ---  ## Important caveats  1. **EBSA coverage is incomplete.** This layer holds **203 EBSAs from 9 of ~15 CBD regional workshops**. Regions **not** represented include the North-East Atlantic, Baltic, Black & Caspian Seas, Seas of East Asia, and North Indian Ocean. The true displacement from *all* CBD-described high-seas EBSAs would therefore be larger.  2. **AIS-tracked, industrial fishing only.** Global Fishing Watch captures vessels carrying AIS transponders. Small-scale/artisanal fleets and vessels that disable AIS are not included.  3. **2024 snapshot.** Effort varies year-to-year; 2024 is the most recent complete year available.  4. **Effort displacement ≠ economic loss.** This is a spatial overlap of fishing activity; it does not estimate catch value, revenue, or where vessels would relocate.  If you’d like, I can also produce a **map of the displaced fishing effort** or break the numbers down by individual EBSA or ocean basin.

## Fishing Displacement Analysis: Closing All High-Seas EBSAs to Fishing  ### Overview  If all EBSAs on the high seas were designated as no-take MPAs, the closure would cover **~43.5 million km²** of ocean beyond national jurisdiction — about **68% of all EBSA area** (total EBSA area = 63.7M km²) and roughly **12% of the global high seas**.  ---  ### Fishing Effort Displaced (2024)  | Gear Type | 2024 Fishing Hours | 5-yr Avg (2020–2024) | Share of Displaced | |---|---:|---:|---:| | **Drifting longlines** | **1,475,980** | **1,499,000** | **60.5%** | | Trawlers | 340,534 | 331,366 | 14.0% | | Squid jiggers | 279,675 | 270,868 | 11.5% | | Unclassified fishing | 196,796 | 205,039 | 8.1% | | Pole and line | 40,774 | 38,088 | 1.7% | | Set longlines | 39,493 | 30,094 | 1.6% | | Other purse seines | 33,344 | 28,575 | 1.4% | | Tuna purse seines | 18,540 | 40,297 | 0.8% | | Fixed gear | 6,699 | 3,904 | 0.3% | | Purse seines (unclassified) | 4,365 | 4,359 | 0.2% | | Set gillnets | 1,915 | 2,027 | 0.1% | | Dredge fishing | 738 | 596 | <0.1% | | Trollers | 461 | 463 | <0.1% | | Pots and traps | 268 | 1,745 | <0.1% | | Seiners | 40 | 48 | <0.1% | | **Total** | **~2,439,000** | **~2,456,000** | **100%** |  ### Context: Share of High-Seas Fishing Displaced  | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Total fishing hours in high-seas EBSAs (2024) | ~2.44 million | | Total fishing hours on all high seas (2024) | ~10.79 million | | **Share of high-seas fishing displaced** | **~22.6%** |  ---  ### Key Findings  1. **Drifting longlines dominate displaced effort** — 1.48M hours (60.5% of all displaced fishing). These are primarily pelagic longline fleets targeting tuna, swordfish, and other large pelagics in the open ocean. This is the single gear type most affected by any high-seas EBSA closure.  2. **Bottom trawling is the second-largest impact** — 340,534 hours (14%). Trawlers on the high seas typically target deep-water species on seamounts, ridges, and plateaus — areas that overlap significantly with EBSAs described for their fragility and benthic biodiversity (e.g., Walvis Ridge, Emperor Seamount Chain, Nazca-Salas y Gómez).  3. **Squid jiggers** contribute 279,675 hours (11.5%), concentrated in high-productivity frontal zones like the North Pacific Transition Zone and the Equatorial Productivity Zone — both among the largest high-seas EBSAs.  4. **The 10 largest high-seas EBSAs account for ~80% of the displaced area:**     | EBSA | High-Seas Area (km²) |    |---|---:|    | North Pacific Transition Zone | 11,108,000 |    | Equatorial High-Productivity Zone | 4,879,000 |    | Agulhas Front | 3,708,000 |    | Sargasso Sea | 3,699,000 |    | Equatorial Productivity Zone | 2,677,000 |    | Subtropical Convergence Zone | 2,182,000 |    | Multi-Year Ice of the Arctic Ocean | 1,815,000 |    | Central Indian Basin | 1,775,000 |    | Marginal Ice Zone (Arctic) | 1,560,000 |    | Hawaiian Albatross Foraging Area | 1,533,000 |  ### Important Caveats  - **EBSA coverage is incomplete.** This layer contains 203 EBSAs from 9 of ~15 CBD regional workshops. It does **not** include the North-East Atlantic, Baltic, Black & Caspian Seas, Seas of East Asia, or North Indian Ocean workshops. The true displaced fishing would be higher if all ~338 described EBSAs were included. - **EBSAs ≠ MPAs.** EBSAs are scientific descriptions with no legal protection. This analysis is a hypothetical scenario — designating them as closed MPAs would require a new legal instrument (e.g., the BBNJ Treaty / High Seas Treaty). - **AIS-based effort underestimates total fishing.** GFW captures only AIS-tracked vessels (>190,000 globally).
